title: Evolution of terahertz third harmonic response across rare-earth nickelate phase-diagram
authors: Gulloo Lal Prajapati, Igor Ilyakov, Alexey Ponomaryov, Atiqa Arshad, Sanjeev Kumar, Jayaprakash Sahoo, Dhanvir Singh Rana, Abdelrahman Azab, Friedemann Queisser, Ralf Schützhold, Jan-Christoph Deinert
abstract: High harmonic generation (HHG) is a sensitive probe for investigating electronic structures and dynamics of materials and a source for attosecond pulses. In particular, HHG with terahertz (THz) light can enable probing of nonlinear responses in correlated materials arising from low-energy many-body interactions. However, THz HHG studies have so far largely focused on topological materials and superconductors, leaving out other potential material systems which could also become efficient THz HHG sources. Here, we report THz third harmonic generation (THG) in rare-earth nickelates -- a prototype material for exploring the Mott insulator-metal transition and related technological applications. We find that the THG amplitude is highly sensitive to the strengths of electronic and magnetic phases of nickelates. In films with sharp phase-transitions, the local maximum and minimum in the temperature-dependent THG amplitude coincide with insulator-metal and magnetic transition temperatures, respectively. While in films with weaker transitions, these features shift toward lower temperatures or even monotonous THG enhancement is observed down to low temperatures. We developed a generalized theory for THz harmonic generation in negative charge-transfer insulators and outlined strategies to enhance the THz nonlinearities further. Our study broadens the scope of THz HHG studies and related applications to strongly correlated materials.
summary: This work demonstrates that Terahertz High Harmonic Generation (THG) is a powerful diagnostic tool for probing the complex phase transitions within rare-earth nickelates, which are model systems for Mott insulators. By measuring THG as a function of temperature and strain, the authors show a direct correlation between the nonlinear optical response and the material's electronic/magnetic state. This broadens the applicability of THz HHG studies to strongly correlated materials.
main_problem: The study investigates nonlinear electronic responses, specifically Terahertz High Harmonic Generation (THG), as a sensitive probe for understanding the Mott insulator-metal transition in strongly correlated materials.
main_result: The THG amplitude is highly sensitive to the electronic and magnetic phases of rare-earth nickelates; local extrema in temperature-dependent THG coincide with known phase transitions like the insulator-metal crossover.
method: The research combines experimental measurements (temperature-dependent THG/resistivity on thin films) with theoretical modeling using generalized theories for harmonic generation in charge-transfer insulators.
model_or_system: The system is rare-earth nickelates (RNiO3), which are modeled as negative charge-transfer insulators exhibiting strong hybridization between Ni 3d and O 2p orbitals. The physics is governed by the competition between electronic correlations, magnetic ordering (AFM/PM), and structural parameters like the Ni-O-Ni bond angle.
key_observables: THz third harmonic generation (THG) amplitude vs. Temperature; Resistivity vs. Temperature; THG/FH ratio.
important_parameters: Rare-earth atomic radius (controlling $	ext{Ni}-	ext{O}-	ext{Ni}$ bond angle); Epitaxial strain (compressive/tensile); Transition temperatures ($T_{MI}, T_N$).
main_assumptions: The THG signal is assumed to be a direct probe of the electronic structure and charge dynamics, allowing for mapping of phase transitions. The theory developed applies to negative charge-transfer insulators.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the RNiO3 phase diagram based on bond angle; temperature dependence plots show THG amplitude coinciding with magnetic and metal-insulator transition temperatures in both bulk and strained film samples.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the utility of THz HHG for correlated materials, details measurements across various nickelate phases (varying R element, strain), presents how THG signals track phase transitions ($T_{MI}, T_N$), develops a generalized theory, and concludes by outlining enhancement strategies.
why_interesting: The use of nonlinear optical techniques like THz HHG to map out complex many-body phase diagrams in correlated oxides provides an experimental handle on phenomena typically studied via transport or spectroscopy alone. The sensitivity to subtle changes in electronic structure makes it valuable for understanding strongly interacting electron systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
